1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}

111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}

114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.

122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.

169	\begin{lemma}

174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write

177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[

181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).

192	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
193	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
194	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
195	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives

263	\begin{lemma}
264	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
265	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
267	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
268	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
269	  \(M\) at the image submonoid

277	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
278	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
279	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
280	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
281	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
282	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the

295	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
296	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
298	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
299	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as

311	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is

354	\begin{lemma}
355	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
356	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
357	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
358	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
359	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
360	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
361	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
362	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
363	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
364	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
365	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
367	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
368	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
369	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
370	  \[
371	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
372	    \;\cong\;
373	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
374	  \]
375	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
376	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
377	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
378	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
379	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
380	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
381	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
382	
383	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
384	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
385	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
386	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
387	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
388	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
389	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
394	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars}
395	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
396	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
397	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
398	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
399	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
400	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
401	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
402	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
403	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
404	
405	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
406	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
407	  \[
408	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
409	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
410	  \]
411	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
412	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
413	
414	  \medskip
415	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
416	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
417	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
418	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
419	
420	  \medskip
421	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
422	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
423	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
424	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
425	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
426	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
427	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
428	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
429	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
430	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
431	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
432	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
433	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
434	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
435	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
436	  an isomorphism.
437	
438	  \medskip
439	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
440	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
441	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
442	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
443	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
444	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
445	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
446	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
447	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
448	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
449	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
450	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
451	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
452	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
453	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
454	
455	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
456	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
457	  isomorphism
458	  \[
459	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
460	      \;\cong\;
461	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
462	  \]
463	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
464	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
465	  section maps, glued by an \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) — except that the
466	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
467	  the \(\top\)-level one of
468	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
469	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
470	  \(\top\).
471	
472	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The equation grounding the
473	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
474	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
475	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
476	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
477	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
478	  \[
479	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
480	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
481	    \;=\;
482	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
483	  \]
484	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
485	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
486	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
487	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
488	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
489	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi).\mathrm{app}(D(a))\).
490	
491	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
492	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) carries the canonical
493	  Mathlib instance exhibiting it as the localization
494	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a))\) over \(R'\). Apply
495	  the ring-change converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra
496	  \(A = R'\) and submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
497	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
498	  rewrite this as an \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,(\mathrm{powers}(a))\) instance,
499	  over \(R\), on the same module by restriction of scalars. Transporting that
500	  instance back across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
501	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields exactly
502	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,(\mathrm{powers}(a))\) on
503	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
504	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
506	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
508	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
509	  global ring map.
510	
511	  \medskip
512	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
513	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
514	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
515	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
516	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
517	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
518	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
519	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
520	  needed for the affine lane.
521	\end{proof}

523	\begin{remark}
524	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
525	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
526	  \begin{enumerate}
527	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
528	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
529	      \(R\)-module of sections of
530	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
531	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
532	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
533	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
534	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
535	      closed under isomorphism; hence
536	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
537	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
538	      needed for the affine lane.
539	  \end{enumerate}
540	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
541	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
542	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
543	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
544	\end{remark}

7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

268	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.

284	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
285	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor

290	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.

293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).

505	  \[

515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and

594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along

599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is

622	  \]

776	  \[

952	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
953	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
954	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
955	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
956	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.

959	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
960	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
961	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
962	  \[
963	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
964	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).

968	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
969	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
